title: Cat Crusty Discharge Around Nostrils: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about cat crusty nose discharge, when it requires a vet visit, and why a photo helps our triage team provide the best care for your pet.
content_html:
What is Crusty Discharge Around a Cat's Nostrils?

Crusty discharge around a cat's nose is often composed of dried mucus, environmental dust, or secretions from the tear ducts. It can range in color from clear or black to yellow and green. While it can sometimes be a sign of a simple respiratory irritant or normal aging, it can also indicate a viral or bacterial infection, such as Feline Herpesvirus or Calicivirus.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for isolated crusty discharge is generally Low. If your cat is otherwise behaving normally—eating, drinking, and playing as usual—this is typically not an immediate emergency. However, you should monitor your pet closely. If the crustiness is accompanied by significant lethargy, a total loss of appetite, or any visible difficulty breathing, the urgency increases, and you should contact your veterinarian promptly.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear, close-up photo of your cat’s nose can be an invaluable tool for your veterinary triage team. A high-quality image allows professionals to evaluate the texture, color, and distribution of the crusting. Seeing the discharge helps the vet determine if the issue is localized to the skin or coming from within the nasal passage, which helps guide the next steps for your cat's comfort and care.

faqs.question: Can I clean the crust off my cat's nose?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can gently use a soft cloth or cotton ball dampened with warm water to soften and wipe away the crusts. Be very gentle to avoid irritating the sensitive skin underneath.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about my cat's runny nose?
faqs.answer: You should seek veterinary care if the discharge changes from clear to yellow or green, if your cat begins sneezing frequently, or if you notice any swelling around the bridge of the nose.

faqs.question: Are 'nose crusties' normal in older cats?
faqs.answer: While small amounts of clear or dark debris can be common, any new or increasing discharge in senior cats should be evaluated to rule out underlying health issues or dental disease.
